content: Progressive resistance training (RT) protocols are necessary to stimulate further adaptation toward specific training goals. It is impossible to improve at the same rate over long-term periods (e.g., 96 months). The proper manipulation of program variables can limit training plateaus and increase the ability to achieve a higher level of muscular fitness. Trainable characteristics include muscular strength, power, hypertrophy, and local muscular endurance (LME). Variables such as speed and agility, balance, coordination, jumping ability, flexibility, and other measures of motor performance may be enhanced by RT. RT, when incorporated into a comprehensive fitness program, improves cardiovascular function. RT reduces the risk factors associated with coronary heart disease. RT reduces the risk factors associated with non–insulin-dependent diabetes. RT prevents osteoporosis. RT may reduce the risk of colon cancer. RT promotes weight loss and maintenance. RT improves dynamic stability and preserves functional capacity. RT fosters psychological well-being.
summary: This chunk outlines the benefits of progressive resistance training (RT) for various aspects of health and fitness, including muscular strength, cardiovascular health, and disease prevention.

content: The foremost principles of RT progression are progressive overload, specificity, and variation. Progressive overload is the gradual increase of stress placed upon the body during exercise training. Systematically increasing the demands placed upon the body is necessary for further improvement and may be accomplished through altering one or more of the following variables: 1) exercise intensity (i.e., absolute or relative resistance/load for a given exercise/movement) may be increased; 2) total repetitions performed at the current intensity may be increased; 3) repetition speed/tempo with submaximal loads may be altered according to goals; 4) rest periods may be shortened for endurance improvements or lengthened for strength and power training; and 5) training volume (total work represented as the product of the total number of repetitions performed and the resistance) may be gradually increased (e.g., 2.5–5% [75]). All training adaptations are specific to the stimulus applied. The specific physiological adaptations to RT are determined by various factors, including 1) muscle actions involved; 2) speed of movement; 3) range of motion; 4) muscle groups trained; 5) energy systems involved; and 6) intensity and volume of training.
summary: This chunk explains the fundamental principles of RT progression: progressive overload, specificity, and variation, detailing how to manipulate training variables for optimal adaptation.

content: The optimal characteristics of strength-specific programs include the use of concentric (CON), eccentric (ECC), and isometric muscle actions and the performance of bilateral and unilateral single- and multiple-joint exercises. It is recommended that strength programs sequence exercises to optimize the preservation of exercise intensity (large before small muscle group exercises, multiple -joint exercises before single-joint exercises, and higher-intensity before lower-intensity exercises). For novice training, it is recommended that loads correspond to a repetition range of an 8–12 repetition maximum (RM). For intermediate to advanced training, it is recommended that individuals use a wider loading range from 1 to 12 RM in a periodized fashion with eventual emphasis on heavy loading (1–6 RM) using 3- to 5-min rest periods between sets performed at a moderate contraction velocity (1–2 s CON; 1–2 s ECC). When training at a specific RM load, it is recommended that a 2–10% increase in load be applied when the individual can perform the current workload for one to two repetitions over the desired number.
summary: This chunk outlines the key characteristics of effective strength training programs, including exercise selection, sequencing, and loading strategies for different training levels.

content: Similar program designs are recommended for hypertrophy training with respect to exercise selection and frequency. For loading, it is recommended that loads corresponding to 1–12 RM be used in periodized fashion with emphasis on the 6–12 RM zone using 1- to 2-min rest periods between sets at a moderate velocity. Higher volume, multiple-set programs are recommended for maximizing hypertrophy.
summary: This chunk details program design considerations for hypertrophy training, emphasizing exercise selection, frequency, loading, and rest periods.

content: Progression in power training entails two general loading strategies: 1) strength training and 2) use of light loads (0–60% of 1 RM for lower body exercises; 30–60% of 1 RM for upper body exercises) performed at a fast contraction velocity with 3–5 min of rest between sets for multiple sets per exercise (three to five sets). It is also recommended that emphasis be placed on multiple-joint exercises especially those involving the total body.
summary: This chunk outlines the recommended training strategies for power development, including strength training and light load, fast velocity exercises.

content: For local muscular endurance training, it is recommended that light to moderate loads (40–60% of 1 RM) be performed for high repetitions (9–15) using short rest periods (G90 s).
summary: This chunk provides guidelines for local muscular endurance training, specifying load, repetitions, and rest periods.

content: The current document replaces the American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) 2002 Position Stand entitled BProgression Models in Resistance Training for Healthy Adults[ (8). The 2002 ACSM Position Stand extended the resistance training (RT) guidelines initially established by the ACSM in the position stand entitled BThe Recommended Quantity and Quality of Exercise for Developing and Maintaining Cardiorespiratory and Muscular Fitness, and Flexibility in Healthy Adults [ (7), which suggested the minimal standard of one set of 8–12 repetitions for 8–10 exercises, including one exercise for all major muscle groups, and 10–15 repetitions for older and more frail persons. Since 2002, numerous studies have been published examining one or more RT variable(s) to support the progressive adaptation in muscular strength and performance. These studies have identified other mechanisms of physiological adaptations and have served to bolster the scientific integrity of the RT knowledge base.
summary: This chunk provides historical context for the current RT guidelines, referencing previous ACSM position stands and highlighting the evolution of knowledge in the field.

content: Although there is some carryover of training effects to other general fitness and performance attributes, the most effective RT programs are those that are designed to target-specific training goals. Variation, or periodization, entails the systematic process of altering one or more program variable(s) over time to allow for the training stimulus to remain challenging and effective. Because the human body adapts quickly to an RT program, at least some changes are needed in order for continual progression to occur. It has been shown that systematic variation of volume and intensity is most effective for long-term progression.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of specificity in RT programming and the need for variation (periodization) to ensure continued progress.

content: The specific physiological adaptations to RT are determined by various factors, including 1) muscle actions involved; 2) speed of movement; 3) range of motion; 4) muscle groups trained; 5) energy systems involved; and 6) intensity and volume of training.
summary: RT adaptations are influenced by factors like muscle actions, speed, range of motion, trained muscle groups, energy systems, and training intensity/volume.

content: Although there is some carryover of training effects to other general fitness and performance attributes, the most effective RT programs are those that are designed to target-specific training goals.
summary: While RT benefits general fitness, the most effective programs are tailored to specific goals.

content: Variation, or periodization, entails the systematic process of altering one or more program variable(s) over time to allow for the training stimulus to remain challenging and effective. Because the human body adapts quickly to an RT program, at least some changes are needed in order for continual progression to occur. It has been shown that systematic variation of volume and intensity is most effective for long-term progression.
summary: Periodization involves systematically changing training variables to maintain challenge and effectiveness.  Variation, especially in volume and intensity, is crucial for long-term progress.

content: The concept of periodization was developed based on the studies of general adaptation syndrome by Hans Selye to optimize performance and recovery. In addition to sport-specific training, periodized RT has been shown to be effective for recreational and rehabilitative objectives and is supported through a meta-analytical investigation to be superior to nonperiodized RT.
summary: Periodization, based on Selye's work, optimizes performance and recovery. It's effective for various goals beyond sports, including recreation and rehabilitation, and is superior to nonperiodized training.

content: The classic (linear) model of periodization is characterized by high initial training volume and low intensity, and as training progresses, volume decreases and intensity gradually increases.
summary: The classic periodization model starts with high volume and low intensity, gradually decreasing volume and increasing intensity over time.

content: A reverse linear periodization model has been studied. This model is the inverse of the classical model in which intensity is initially at its highest and volume at its lowest. Subsequently, over an extended time, intensity decreases and volume increases with each phase.
summary: Reverse linear periodization starts with high intensity and low volume, gradually decreasing intensity and increasing volume over time.

content: This periodization model has been used for individuals targeting local muscular endurance (LME) enhancement and was shown to be superior for enhancing LME to other periodization models when volume and intensity were equated. Strength improvements following this model have been shown to be lower compared with linear and undulating models.
summary: Reverse linear periodization is effective for local muscular endurance but less so for strength compared to linear and undulating models.

content: The undulating (nonlinear) model of periodization enables variation in intensity and volume within a cycle by rotating different protocols to train various components of neuromuscular performance. For example, in loading schemes for core exercises, the use of heavy, moderate, and light resistances may be systematically or randomly rotated over a training sequence.
summary: The undulating model varies intensity and volume within a cycle, using different protocols to target various neuromuscular components.

content: This model has compared favorably with linear periodized and nonperiodized multiple-set models and has been shown to produce superior strength increases over 12 wk of RT compared with the classical model. Further, this model has demonstrated advantages in comparison to nonperiodized, low-volume training in women. Few investigations have evaluated the impact of undulating RT for multiple fitness objectives. Most recently, this model has been demonstrated superior over nonundulating RT for generating fitness and performance enhancement outcomes among firefighter trainees.
summary: The undulating model has shown superior strength gains compared to linear and nonperiodized models, particularly in women. It's also effective for firefighter trainees.

content: The squat targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: The squat primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. Deep squats enhance glute activation, and a typical strength prescription involves 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.

content: Important to periodization is the use of rest days to allow recovery and to reduce the probability or magnitude of overtraining. Longer training periods (96 months) may be necessary to underscore the benefits of periodized training because periodized and nonperiodized training are effective during short-term training.
summary: Rest days are crucial for recovery and preventing overtraining. While both periodized and nonperiodized training are effective in the short term, longer periods (96 months) may be needed to fully realize the benefits of periodization.

content: The ability to generate force is necessary for all types of movement. Muscle fiber cross-sectional area (CSA) is positively related to maximal force production. The arrangement of fibers according to their angle of pennation, muscle length, joint angle, and contraction velocity can alter the expression of muscular strength.
summary: Force generation is essential for movement. Muscle fiber CSA positively correlates with force production, and factors like fiber arrangement, muscle length, joint angle, and contraction velocity influence strength.

content: Force generation is further dependent upon motor unit activation, and motor units are recruited according to their recruitment threshold that typically involves the activation of the slower (lower force-producing) motor units before the faster (higher force-producing) units, that is, size principle.
summary: Force generation relies on motor unit activation, which follows the size principle, recruiting slower motor units first, followed by faster ones.

content: Adaptations to RT enable greater force generation through numerous neuromuscular mechanisms. Muscle strength may increase significantly within the first week of training. Long-term strength enhancement manifests itself through enhanced neural function (e.g., greater recruitment, rate of discharge), increased muscle CSA, changes in muscle architecture, and possible adaptations to increased metabolites, for example, H + , for increased strength.
summary: RT adaptations enhance force generation through various neuromuscular mechanisms. Strength gains occur quickly initially, with long-term improvements involving neural adaptations, muscle growth, and metabolic changes.

content: The magnitude of strength enhancement is dependent on the type of program used and the careful prescription of muscle actions, intensity, volume, exercise selection and order, rest periods between sets, and frequency.
summary: Strength gains depend on program type, intensity, volume, exercise selection, rest periods, and frequency.

content: Most RT programs primarily include dynamic repetitions with both concentric (CON; muscle shortening) and eccentric (ECC; muscle lengthening) muscle actions, whereas isometric (ISOM; no net change in muscle length) actions play a secondary role.
summary: Most RT programs focus on dynamic CON and ECC actions, with ISOM actions playing a lesser role.

content: Greater force per unit of muscle size is produced during ECC actions than either CON or ISOM actions. Moreover, ECC actions require less motor unit activation per specific load, are less metabolically demanding, and are conducive to promoting hypertrophic adaptation yet result in more pronounced delayed onset muscle soreness as compared with CON actions.
summary: ECC actions produce more force per muscle unit, require less motor unit activation, are less metabolically taxing, promote hypertrophy, but cause more muscle soreness than CON actions.

content: Dynamic CON muscular strength improvement is greatest when ECC actions are included with CON actions, and independently, ECC isokinetic training has been shown to produce greater muscle action-specific strength gains than CON training.
summary: Combining CON and ECC actions maximizes CON strength gains, and ECC training specifically enhances muscle action-specific strength.

content: The role of muscle action manipulation during RT is minimal with respect to overall progression because most programs include both CON and ECC actions in a given repetition. However, the inclusion of additional ISOM exercise may be beneficial.
summary: While most programs already incorporate both CON and ECC actions, adding more ISOM exercises might be beneficial.

content: Greater force per unit of muscle size is produced during ECC actions than either CON or ISOM actions. Moreover, ECC actions require less motor unit activation per specific load, are less metabolically demanding, and are conducive to promoting hypertrophic adaptation yet result in more pronounced delayed onset muscle soreness as compared with CON actions. Dynamic CON muscular strength improvement is greatest when ECC actions are included with CON actions, and independently, ECC isokinetic training has been shown to produce greater muscle action-specific strength gains than CON training.
summary: ECC actions produce greater force per muscle unit, require less motor unit activation, and are more conducive to hypertrophy than CON actions, but cause more soreness.

content: The role of muscle action manipulation during RT is minimal with respect to overall progression because most programs include both CON and ECC actions in a given repetition. However, the inclusion of additional ISOM exercise may be beneficial. In some programs, the use of different forms of ISOM training, for example, functional ISOM and supramaximal ECC actions, has been reported to produce additional benefit. Specifically, certain ISOM actions have been recommended for promoting low back health and have been demonstrated effective for the selective recruitment of postural, spinal-stabilization musculature.
summary: While most RT programs already incorporate CON and ECC actions, additional ISOM exercises, especially functional ISOM and supramaximal ECC, can provide further benefits, particularly for low back health.

content: For progression during RT for novice, intermediate, and advanced individuals, it is recommended that CON, ECC, and ISOM muscle actions be included.
summary: Progression in RT should include CON, ECC, and ISOM muscle actions for all experience levels.

content: Proper loading during resistance training (RT) encompasses one or more of the following loading schemes: increasing load based on a percentage of 1 RM, increasing absolute load based on a targeted repetition number, or increasing loading within a prescribed zone (e.g., 8–12 RM). Loads of 45–50% of 1 RM (and less) have been shown to increase dynamic muscular strength in previously untrained individuals. Light loads that can be lifted a maximum of 15–25 repetitions have been shown to increase strength in moderately trained individuals. At least 80% of 1 RM is needed to produce further neural adaptations and strength during RT in experienced lifters.
summary: Proper loading schemes for RT include percentage of 1RM, absolute load based on repetitions, or loading within a prescribed zone. Load selection depends on training experience and goals.

content: Several pioneering studies indicated that training with loads corresponding to 1–6 RM (mostly 5–6 RM) was most conducive to increasing maximal dynamic strength. Strength increases have been shown to be greater using heavy weights for 3–5 RM compared with 9–11 and 20–28 RM. Although significant strength increases have been reported using loads corresponding to 8–12 RM and lighter, this loading range may be inferior for maximizing strength in advanced lifters. Meta-analytical data have shown that 60% of 1 RM produced the largest effect sizes for strength increases in novice individuals whereas 80% of 1 RM produced the largest effect sizes for strength increases in trained individuals and 85% of 1 RM was most effective in athletes.
summary: Training with loads of 1-6 RM, particularly 5-6 RM, is most effective for maximal strength gains. Heavier loads (3-5 RM) produce greater strength increases than lighter loads (9-11, 20-28 RM). Optimal load percentages vary by training experience, with 60% for novices, 80% for trained individuals, and 85% for athletes.

content: For novice individuals, it has been suggested that moderate loading (50–60% of 1 RM or less) be used initially as learning proper form, and technique is paramount. Recent studies have shown that self-selected RT intensities are lower than what is recommended, for example, 38–58% of 1 RM. It is recommended that novice to intermediate individuals train with loads corresponding to 60–70% of 1 RM for 8–12 repetitions and advanced individuals cycle training loads of 80–100% of 1 RM to maximize muscular strength. For progression in those individuals training at a specific RM load, it is recommended that a 2–10% (lower percent for small muscle mass exercises, higher percent increase for large muscle mass exercises) increase in load be applied when the individual can perform the current workload for one to two repetitions over the desired number on two consecutive training sessions.
summary: Load selection for RT should be individualized based on experience level. Novices should prioritize proper form with moderate loads, while intermediate and advanced individuals should progressively increase loads to maximize strength gains.

content: Training volume is a summation of the total number of repetitions performed during a training session multiplied by the resistance used (kg) and is reflective of the duration of which muscles are being stressed. Altering training volume can be accomplished by changing the number of exercises performed per session, the number of repetitions performed per set, or the number of sets per exercise. Studies using two (55,170), three (149,250), four to five (56,122), and six or more (123,236) sets per exercise have all produced significant increases in muscular strength in both trained and untrained individuals.
summary: Training volume, calculated as repetitions x resistance, reflects the duration of muscle stress. It can be adjusted by changing the number of exercises, repetitions per set, or sets per exercise. Studies show significant strength gains across various sets per exercise.

content: A meta-analysis of 37 studies has shown that approximately eight sets per muscle group produced the largest effect size in athletes. Several studies have reported similar strength increases between single- and multiple-set programs, whereas others reported multiple-set programs superior in previously untrained individuals. Multiple-set programs formed the basis for the popularity of single-set training among general fitness enthusiasts. In resistance-trained individuals, multiple-set programs have been shown to be superior for strength enhancement in all but one study. Among resistance-trained postmenopausal women, multiple-set training led to 3.5–5.5% strength increases, whereas single-set training led to 1% to 2% strength reductions. Meta-analytical studies have shown multiple-set RT superior to single sets for strength enhancement in untrained and trained populations. These studies have shown that performing three to four sets per exercise produced the most substantial effect sizes. It is recommended that one to three sets per exercise be used by novice individuals initially. For progression into intermediate to advanced status, data from long-term studies indicate that multiple sets be used with systematic variation of volume and intensity over time.
summary: Multiple-set programs generally produce greater strength gains than single-set programs, particularly for trained individuals and athletes. However, single-set programs can be effective for beginners. As individuals progress, multiple sets with varying volume and intensity are recommended.

content: Both single- and multiple-joint exercises have been shown to be effective for increasing muscular strength in the targeted muscle groups using multiple modalities. Multiple-joint exercises, such as bench press and squat, require complex neural responses and have generally been regarded more effective for increasing overall muscular strength because they enable a greater magnitude of weight to be lifted. Single-joint exercises, such as knee extensions and knee curls, have been used to target specific muscle groups and pose a reduced level of skill and technical involvement.
summary: Both single- and multiple-joint exercises are effective for strength gains, but multiple-joint exercises, like bench press and squat, are generally considered more effective for overall strength due to their greater weight capacity and complex neural demands.

content: Unilateral training may increase bilateral strength (in addition to unilateral strength), and bilateral training may increase unilateral strength. Unilateral training has been shown to improve some aspects of sports performance, such as single-leg jumping ability to a greater extent than bilateral training. Exercises performed in unstable environments have been shown to increase the activity of lower torso musculature and other stabilizer muscles compared with stable environments; however, the magnitude of agonist force production is considerably lower resulting in lighter weights lifted.
summary: Both unilateral and bilateral training offer benefits, with unilateral training potentially enhancing single-leg performance. Unstable environments increase stabilizer muscle activity but may limit overall weight lifted.

content: Unilateral and bilateral single- and multiple-joint exercises should be included in RT with emphasis on multiple-joint exercises for maximizing overall muscle strength in novice, intermediate, and advanced individuals. Weight machines have been regarded as safer to use, easy to learn, and allow performance of some exercises that may be difficult with free weights.
summary: RT programs should incorporate both unilateral and bilateral exercises, prioritizing multiple-joint exercises for overall strength gains. Weight machines offer safety, ease of use, and access to exercises not easily performed with free weights.

content: Unilateral training may increase bilateral strength (in addition to unilateral strength), and bilateral training may increase unilateral strength. Unilateral training has been shown to improve some aspects of sports performance, such as single-leg jumping ability to a greater extent than bilateral training.
summary: Unilateral and bilateral training offer benefits for both strength and sports performance.

content: Exercises performed in unstable environments have been shown to increase the activity of lower torso musculature and other stabilizer muscles compared with stable environments; however, the magnitude of agonist force production is considerably lower resulting in lighter weights lifted.
summary: Unstable exercise environments engage more stabilizer muscles, but may reduce agonist force production.

content: Unilateral and bilateral single- and multiple-joint exercises should be included in RT with emphasis on multiple-joint exercises for maximizing overall muscle strength in novice, intermediate, and advanced individuals.
summary: Resistance training (RT) should incorporate both unilateral and bilateral exercises, prioritizing multi-joint movements for optimal strength gains.

content: Weight machines have been regarded as safer to use, easy to learn, and allow performance of some exercises that may be difficult with free weights. Machines help stabilize the body and limit movement about specific joints involved in synergistic force production, and machine exercises have demonstrated less neural activation when matched for intensity for most comparisons to free-weight exercises.
summary: Weight machines offer safety, ease of use, and specific joint stabilization, but may engage less neural activation compared to free weights.

content: Free-weight training leads to greater improvements in free-weight tests and machine training results in greater performance on machine tests. When a neutral testing device is used, strength improvement from free weights and machines appears similar.
summary: Free-weight training generally results in better free-weight strength gains, while machine training improves machine-specific performance. Neutral testing suggests similar strength gains with both methods.

content: For novice to intermediate training, it is recommended that free-weight and machine exercises are included.
summary: Both free-weight and machine exercises should be incorporated into novice and intermediate training programs.

content: Another study comparing very slow [(10:5)] to traditional velocity [(1:1)] showed that 37–40% reductions in training loads were needed to attain the same number of repetitions (129). These data suggest that motor unit activity may be limited when intentionally slow velocities at lighter loads are incorporated and ultimately may not provide an optimal stimulus for strength enhancement in resistance-trained individuals.
summary: Training with very slow velocities may require significantly heavier loads to achieve the same repetitions as traditional velocities, potentially limiting motor unit activation and strength gains.

content: Compared with slow velocities, moderate [(1–2:1–2)] and fast [(G1:1)] velocities have been shown to be more effective for enhanced muscular performance capacities (e.g., number of repetitions performed, work and power output, and volume) (161,189) and for increasing the rate of strength gains (113). The number of repetitions performed is based upon a continuum depending on the lifting velocity where the largest numbers of repetitions are performed with a fast velocity and decreases proportionately as velocity becomes slower (234). The effect of lifting velocity on repetition performance appears largest with light to moderately heavy loading (234).
summary: Moderate and fast lifting velocities generally lead to greater muscular performance and strength gains compared to slow velocities, with the number of repetitions increasing with faster velocities.

content: Most advanced RT studies examining fast velocities with moderately high intensities have shown these velocities to be more effective than traditionally slower velocities for strength increases (133,190). It appears that the intent to maximally accelerate the weight during training is critical in maximizing strength gains (19). Although loading may be moderate to heavy, the intent to lift the weight as fast as possible has been shown to be critical for maximizing strength increases (19).
summary: For advanced training, maximizing lifting velocity with moderate to heavy loads is crucial for optimal strength gains.

content: Keeler et al. (141) showed that traditional velocity [(2:4)] RT produced significantly greater strength increases over 10 wk than super slow training in five of eight exercises trained (overall increase of 39% vs 15% in traditional and super slow, respectively). Over 6 wk of RT in untrained individuals, it was shown that training at a faster velocity [(1:1)] led to È11% greater strength increases than training at a slower velocity [(3:3)] (192). However, a study by Neils et al. (195) showed statistically similar increases in strength between super slow and slow-velocity [(2:4)] training.
summary: Traditional velocity training generally leads to greater strength gains compared to super slow training, but some studies show similar results between slow and super slow velocities.

content: For untrained individuals, it is recommended that slow and moderate velocities be used (113,141,161,189,192,195). For intermediate training, it is recommended that moderate velocity be used for RT (113,141,161,189,192,195). For advanced training, the inclusion of a continuum of velocities from unintentionally slow to fast velocities is recommended. The velocity selected should correspond to the intensity and the intent should be to maximize the velocity of the CON muscle action (19,133).
summary: Optimal lifting velocity recommendations vary based on training experience, with untrained individuals benefiting from slow and moderate velocities, intermediate individuals using moderate velocities, and advanced individuals incorporating a range of velocities based on intensity and training goals.

content: Optimal RT frequency (the number of workouts per week) depends upon several factors such as volume, intensity, exercise selection, level of conditioning, recovery ability, and number of muscle groups trained per workout session. Numerous studies have used frequencies of two to three alternating days per week in previously untrained individuals (34,44,56,116). This frequency has been shown to be an effective initial frequency, whereas 1–2 d/wk appears to be an effective maintenance frequency for those individuals already engaged in RT (93).
summary: Optimal training frequency depends on individual factors like volume, intensity, and recovery ability, with 2-3 days per week being effective for beginners and 1-2 days for maintenance.

content: In several studies comparing strength gains, 1) 3 d of training per week was superior to 1 (183) and 2 d (94), 2) 3 d produced similar strength increases to 2 d/wk when volume was equated (34), 3) 4 d/wk was superior to three (127), 4) 2 d/wk was superior to 1 (217), and 5) 3–5 d/wk was superior to 1 and 2 d (85). Meta-analytical data have shown that strength gains in untrained individuals were highest with a frequency of 3 d/wk (225).
summary: Research suggests that 3 days per week of training is generally optimal for strength gains in untrained individuals, with other frequencies showing varying results depending on volume and training experience.

content: It is recommended that novice individuals train the entire body 2–3 d/wk (34,44,56,94,116,183,225). It appears that progression from untrained to intermediate training does not necessitate a change in frequency for training each muscle group but may be more dependent upon alterations in other acute variables such as exercise selection, volume, and intensity.
summary: Novice individuals should train the entire body 2-3 times per week, and progression to intermediate training may involve adjustments to exercise selection, volume, and intensity rather than frequency changes.

content: Increasing frequency enables greater specialization (e.g., greater exercise selection and volume per muscle group in accordance with more specific goals). Upper/lower body split or muscle group split routines are common at this level in addition to total-body workouts (157). Similar increases in strength have been observed between upper/lower- and total-body workouts (32).
summary: Intermediate training often utilizes split routines or total-body workouts, allowing for greater specialization and volume per muscle group.

content: It is recommended that for progression to intermediate training, a frequency of 3–4 d/wk be used (3 d if using a total-body workout, 4 d if using a split routine thereby training each major muscle group twice) (34,85,94,183,225).
summary: Intermediate training typically involves 3-4 days per week, with total-body workouts at 3 days and split routines at 4 days.

content: Optimal progression of frequency during advanced training varies considerably. It has been shown that football players training 4–5 d/wk achieved better results than those who trained either 3 or 6 d/wk (118). Advanced and elite weightlifters and bodybuilders use high-frequency training, for example, four to six sessions per week or more. Double-split routines (two training sessions per day with emphasis on different muscle groups) are common during training (102), which may result in 8–12 training sessions per week. Frequencies as high as 18 sessions per week have been reported in elite Olympic weightlifters (280).
summary: Advanced training frequency is highly individualized, with elite athletes often training 4-6 days per week or more, utilizing double-split routines and reaching up to 18 sessions per week.

content: The rationale for high-frequency training is that frequent short sessions followed by periods of recovery, nutrition supplementation, and food intake allow for high-intensity training and performance (reduced fatigue). Ha ¨kkinen and Kallinen (103) reported greater increases in muscle cross-sectional area (CSA) and strength when training volume was divided into two sessions per day as opposed to one.
summary: High-frequency training, common in elite athletes, involves frequent short sessions with adequate recovery and nutrition to maximize training intensity and performance.

content: Elite power lifters train 4–6 d/wk (75).
summary: Elite powerlifters typically train 4-6 days per week.

content: Advanced and elite weightlifters and bodybuilders use high-frequency training, for example, four to six sessions per week or more. Double-split routines (two training sessions per day with emphasis on different muscle groups) are common during training (102), which may result in 8–12 training sessions per week. Frequencies as high as 18 sessions per week have been reported in elite Olympic weightlifters (280). The rationale for high-frequency training is that frequent short sessions followed by periods of recovery, nutrition supplementation, and food intake allow for high-intensity training and performance (reduced fatigue). Ha ¨kkinen and Kallinen (103) reported greater increases in muscle cross-sectional area (CSA) and strength when training volume was divided into two sessions per day as opposed to one. Elite power lifters train 4–6 d/wk (75). It is important to note that not all muscle groups are trained per workout during a high-frequency model of training. Meta-analytical data have shown that training a muscle group two times per week in advanced individuals yielded the highest effect size (225). It is recommended that advanced lifters train 4–6 days per week. Elite weightlifters and bodybuilders may benefit from using very high frequency, for example, two workouts in 1 day for 4–5 days per week.
summary: High-frequency training, with 4-6 sessions per week or more, is common in advanced lifters and may be beneficial for maximizing training volume and recovery.

content: Resistance training (RT) induces muscular hypertrophy through mechanical, metabolic, and hormonal processes. The process of hypertrophy involves a proportionate increase in the net accretion of the contractile proteins actin and myosin as well as other structural proteins. Mechanical loading leads to a series of intracellular events that ultimately regulates gene expression and protein synthesis. RT may alter the activity of nearly 70 genes, up-regulate factors involved with myogenesis (e.g., myogenin, MyoD), and down-regulate inhibitory growth factors (e.g., myostatin). Protein synthesis in human skeletal muscle increases after only one bout of vigorous RT. This anabolic environment remains elevated from 2 to 3 hours postexercise up through 36–48 hours postexercise. Other factors such as fiber type, muscle action, metabolite formation, amino acid intake, and endocrine responses (testosterone, growth hormone [GH], cortisol, insulin, and insulin-like growth factor I) contribute to the magnitude of hypertrophy.
summary: Resistance training induces hypertrophy through mechanical, metabolic, and hormonal pathways, leading to increased muscle protein synthesis and growth.

content: Optimal hypertrophy may comprise maximizing the combination of mechanical (use of heavy weights, eccentric actions, and low to moderate volume) and metabolic (accumulation of metabolic waste products) stimuli. Neural adaptations predominate during the early stages of training. Muscle hypertrophy becomes evident within the first 6 weeks. Changes in the quality of proteins and protein synthesis rates take place much earlier than muscle hypertrophy. Less muscle mass is recruited during training with a given workload once adaptation has taken place. Progressive overloading is necessary for maximal muscle fiber recruitment and, consequently, muscle fiber hypertrophy.
summary: Maximizing both mechanical and metabolic stimuli during training is key for optimal hypertrophy. Neural adaptations occur initially, followed by muscle hypertrophy within 6 weeks.

content: It is recommended that concentric (CON), eccentric (ECC), and isometric (ISOM) muscle actions be included for novice, intermediate, and advanced RT. Similar increases in lean body mass have been shown between single- and multiple-set training. Many of these studies in previously untrained individuals have demonstrated that general, nonspecific program design is effective for increasing hypertrophy in novice to intermediate individuals. Manipulation of acute program variables to optimize both the mechanical and the metabolic factors (using several loading/volume schemes) appears to be the most effective way to optimize hypertrophy during advanced stages of training.
summary: Incorporating various muscle actions (concentric, eccentric, isometric) and training program designs is recommended for all training levels, with advanced lifters benefiting from manipulating program variables to optimize hypertrophy.

content: RT programs targeting muscle hypertrophy have used moderate to very high loading, relatively high volume, and short rest intervals. These programs have been shown to induce a greater acute elevation in testosterone and GH than high-load, low-volume programs with long (3 min) rest periods. Total work, in combination with mechanical loading, has been implicated for both gains in strength and hypertrophy. The addition of one set per exercise consisting of light loading for 25–35 repetitions led to increased muscle CSA whereas conventional strength training alone did not increase muscle CSA. It appears that the combination of strength training (emphasizing mechanical loading) and hypertrophy training, that is, moderate loading, high repetitions, short rest intervals, which emphasizes total work (and reliance upon glycolysis and metabolic factors), is most effective for advanced hypertrophy training.
summary: Effective hypertrophy programs utilize moderate to high loading, volume, and short rest intervals, maximizing both mechanical and metabolic stimuli.

content: For novice and intermediate individuals, it is recommended that moderate loading be used (70–85% of 1 RM) for 8–12 repetitions per set for one to three sets per exercise. It is recommended that single- and multiple-joint free-weight and machine exercises be included in an RT program in novice, intermediate, and advanced individuals. For exercise sequencing, an order similar to strength training is recommended.
summary: Novice and intermediate individuals should use moderate loading (70-85% of 1RM) for 8-12 reps per set, incorporating both single and multiple-joint exercises.

content: The amount of rest between sets and exercises significantly affects the metabolic and hormonal responses to an acute bout of resistance exercise. One study reported no significant difference between 30-, 90-, and 180-s rest intervals in muscle girth, skinfolds, or body mass in recreationally trained men over 5 wk. Ahtiainen et al. (3) showed that 3 months of training with 5-min rest intervals produced a similar increase in muscle CSA to training with 2-min rest intervals. Short rest periods (1–2 min) coupled with moderate to high intensity and volume have elicited the greatest acute anabolic hormonal response in comparison to programs utilizing very heavy loads with long rest periods. It appears a range of rest intervals may be used effectively to target hypertrophy depending on training intensity. It is recommended that 1- to 2-min rest periods be used in novice and intermediate training programs. For advanced training, rest period length should correspond to the goals of each exercise or training phase such that 2- to 3-min rest periods may be used with heavy loading for core exercises and 1–2 min may be used for other exercises of moderate to moderately high intensity.
summary: Rest periods significantly influence hormonal and metabolic responses to training. While shorter rest periods (1-2 minutes) are generally recommended for hypertrophy, rest duration should be tailored to individual training goals and exercise intensity.

content: It appears a range of rest intervals may be used effectively to target hypertrophy depending on training intensity. It is recommended that 1- to 2-min rest periods be used in novice and intermediate training programs. For advanced training, rest period length should correspond to the goals of each exercise or training training phase such that 2- to 3-min rest periods may be used with heavy loading for core exercises and 1–2 min may be used for other exercises of moderate to moderately high intensity.
summary: Rest periods for hypertrophy training should be tailored to training intensity and experience level, ranging from 1-2 minutes for novices and intermediates to 2-3 minutes for advanced lifters with heavy loads.

content: Ahtiainen et al. (3) showed that 3 months of training with 5-min rest intervals produced a similar increase in muscle CSA to training with 2-min rest intervals. Short rest periods (1–2 min) coupled with moderate to high intensity and volume have elicited the greatest acute anabolic hormonal response in comparison to programs utilizing very heavy loads with long rest periods.
summary: Rest interval duration for hypertrophy training can vary, with shorter intervals (1-2 minutes) potentially maximizing hormonal response, while longer intervals (5 minutes) still yield muscle growth.

content: It does appear that the use of different velocities is warranted for long-term improvements in hypertrophy for advanced training. It is recommended that slow to moderate velocities be used by novice- and intermediate-trained individuals. For advanced training, it is recommended that slow, moderate, and fast repetition velocities be used depending on the load, the repetition number, and the goals of the particular exercise.
summary: Repetition velocity for hypertrophy training should be adjusted based on experience level, with novices and intermediates using slow to moderate velocities, while advanced lifters can incorporate a variety of velocities depending on the exercise and goals.

content: 8 wk of fast (210 -Isj1) ECC isokinetic training produced larger increases in Type II muscle fiber CSA than slow (20 -Isj1) training. 8 wk of fast ECC (180 -Isj1) isokinetic training produced greater hypertrophy than slow ECC (30 -Isj1), fast and slow CON training. For dynamic constant external RT, it has been suggested that higher velocities of movement pose less of a stimulus for hypertrophy than slow and moderate velocities. Intentional slow velocities require significant reductions in loading and result in less of a blood lactate response and less metabolic response when total training time is equated.
summary: Studies suggest that faster repetition velocities (e.g., 210 -Isj1) may be more effective for hypertrophy than slower velocities (e.g., 20 -Isj1), particularly for Type II muscle fibers. However, dynamic constant external resistance training with higher velocities may be less effective for hypertrophy compared to slower velocities.

content: Frequencies of 2–3 d Iwkj1 have been effective in novice and intermediate men and women. Higher frequency of RT has been suggested for advanced hypertrophy training. It is recommended that a frequency of 2–3 dIwkj1 be used for novice training (when training the total body each workout). For intermediate training, the recommendation is similar for total-body workouts or 4 dIwkj1 when using an upper/lower body split routine (each major muscle group trained twice per week). For advanced training, a frequency of 4–6 d Iwkj1 is recommended.
summary: Training frequency for hypertrophy should be adjusted based on experience level, with novices and intermediates training 2-3 times per week, while advanced lifters may benefit from 4-6 training days per week.

content: Muscle group split routines (one to three muscle groups trained per workout) are common enabling higher volume per muscle group. Maximal muscle power is demonstrated as the highest power output attainable during a given movement/repetition and has been viewed as an exceedingly important testing variable and training objective. Neuromuscular contributions to maximal muscle power include 1) maximal rate of force development (RFD), 2) force production at slow and fast contraction velocities, 3) stretch-shortening cycle performance, and 4) coordination of movement pattern and skill.
summary: Muscle group split routines allow for higher training volume per muscle group. Maximal muscle power is a key training objective influenced by factors like rate of force development, force production at various velocities, stretch-shortening cycle performance, and movement coordination.

content: Several studies have shown improved power performance following traditional RT (1,88,156,277), demonstrating the reliance of power production on muscular force development. Programs consisting of movements with high power output using relatively light loads have been shown to be superior for improving vertical jump ability than traditional strength training (98,99). Considering that power is the product of force and velocity, it appears that heavy RT with slow velocities improves maximal force production whereas power training (utilizing light to moderate loads at high velocities) increases force output at higher velocities and RFD (98,99).
summary: Traditional resistance training can improve power performance, highlighting the importance of muscular force development. Power training with light to moderate loads and high velocities is more effective for improving vertical jump ability compared to traditional strength training.

content: Heavy RT could decrease power output over time unless accompanied by explosive movements (25). The inherent problem with traditional weight training is that the load is decelerated for a considerable proportion (24–40%) of the CON movement (60,197). This percentage increases to 52% when performing the lift with a lower percentage (81%) of 1 RM lifted (60) or when attempting to move the bar rapidly in an effort to train more specifically near the movement speed of the target activity (197). Ballistic resistance exercise (explosive movements which enable acceleration throughout the full range of motion resulting in greater peak and average lifting velocities) has been shown to limit this problem (48,121,198,276).
summary: Traditional weight training can limit power development due to the deceleration of the load. Ballistic resistance exercise, which emphasizes acceleration throughout the range of motion, can mitigate this issue.

content: Loaded jump squats with 30% of 1 RM have been shown to increase vertical jump performance more than traditional back squats and plyometrics (276). Although single-joint exercises have been studied, multiple-joint exercises have been used extensively for power training (139). The inclusion of total-body exercises (e.g., power clean and push press) is recommended as these exercises have been shown to require rapid force production (82) and be very effective for enhancing power (263).
summary: Loaded jump squats and multiple-joint exercises, such as power cleans and push presses, are effective for improving power output and vertical jump performance.

content: It is recommended that these exercises be performed early in a workout and sequenced based on complexity (e.g., snatch before power cleans and variations such as high pulls). Additionally, performing high-velocity power exercises before a multiple-joint exercise such as the squat has been shown to improve squat performance (247), for example, via postactivation potentiation. The use of predominately multiple-joint exercises performed with sequencing guidelines similar to strength training is recommended for novice, intermediate, and advanced power training (82,139,247,263).
summary: Power exercises should be performed early in the workout, sequenced based on complexity, and potentially precede multiple-joint exercises for enhanced performance.

content: The intensity of which peak power is attained has been variable and shown to be dependent on the type of exercise, whether it is ballistic or traditional, and the strength level of the individual (139). Peak power during ballistic exercises has been shown to range between 15% and 50% (upper body exercises), from 0% (body weight) to 60% (lower body exercises, primarily the jump squat), and peak power for traditional exercises ranges between 30% and 70% of 1 RM (41–43,139,260). Peak power for the Olympic lifts typically occurs approximately 70–80% of 1 RM (42,140).
summary: Peak power attainment during exercises varies depending on exercise type, intensity, and individual strength level. Ballistic exercises generally reach peak power at lower percentages of 1RM compared to traditional exercises.

content: Although any intensity can enhance muscle power and shift the force-velocity curve to the right, specificity is needed such that training encompasses a range of intensities but emphasis placed upon the intensities that match the demands of the sport or activities performed (139). Fast lifting velocities are needed to optimize power development with submaximal loading, and the intent to maximally lift the weight fast is critical when a higher intensity is used (19).
summary: While any intensity can improve power, training should emphasize intensities that match the demands of the specific sport or activity. Fast lifting velocities are crucial for optimizing power development, especially with submaximal loads.

content: The intensity of which peak power is attained has been variable and shown to be dependent on the type of exercise, whether it is ballistic or traditional, and the strength level of the individual (139). Peak power during ballistic exercises has been shown to range between 15% and 50% (upper body exercises), from 0% (body weight) to 60% (lower body exercises, primarily the jump squat), and peak power for traditional exercises ranges between 30% and 70% of 1 RM (41–43,139,260). Peak power for the Olympic lifts typically occurs approximately 70–80% of 1 RM (42,140).
summary: Peak power output varies based on exercise type (ballistic vs. traditional) and intensity, ranging from 0% to 60% of 1RM for ballistic exercises and 30% to 70% for traditional exercises.

content: Although any intensity can enhance muscle power and shift the force-velocity curve to the right, specificity is needed such that training encompasses a range of intensities but emphasis placed upon the intensities that match the demands of the sport or activities performed (139). It is recommended that concurrent to a typical strength training program, a power component is incorporated consisting of one to three sets per exercise using light to moderate loading (30–60% of 1 RM for upper body exercises, 0–60% of 1 RM for lower body exercises) for three to six repetitions (19,41–43,139,260).
summary: Power training should encompass a range of intensities, but emphasize those matching the demands of the sport. A recommended power component includes 1-3 sets of 3-6 reps at 30-60% of 1RM.

content: Progression for power enhancement uses various loading strategies in a periodized manner. Heavy loading (85–100% of 1 RM) is necessary for increasing the force component of the power equation, and light to moderate loading (30–60% of 1 RM for upper body exercises, 0–60% of 1 RM for lower body exercises) performed at an explosive velocity is necessary for increasing fast force production. A multiple-set (three to six sets) power program be integrated into a strength training program consisting of one to six repetitions in a periodized manner is recommended (74,199,206).
summary: Power progression involves periodized loading strategies. Heavy lifting (85-100% 1RM) builds force, while light to moderate loading (30-60% 1RM) at explosive velocities enhances fast force production.

content: Rest period length for power training is similar to strength training. It is recommended that rest periods of at least 2–3 min between sets for core exercises are recommended. A shorter rest interval (1–2 min) is recommended for assistance exercises.
summary: Rest periods for power training are similar to strength training, with 2-3 minutes for core exercises and 1-2 minutes for assistance exercises.

content: Taking the needed rest is vital to ensure the quality of each repetition being performed in a set (achieving a high percent of peak velocity and achieving a high percentage of maximal power output). In addition to the technical quality of each repetition performed in a power training program, accentuated rest periods are also needed for preservation of the appropriate training intensity to occur, which will elicit the desired neurological response.
summary: Adequate rest is crucial for power training, ensuring high repetition quality and maintaining training intensity for optimal neurological response.

content: Power training is typically integrated into a periodized strength training program due to the important inherent relationships between the two variables.
summary: Power training is often integrated into a periodized strength training program due to their interconnected nature.

content: Evidence category A: The recommended frequency for novice power training is 2–3 days per week, stressing the total body. Evidence category B: For intermediate power training, it is recommended that either a total-body or an upper/lower-body split workout be used for a frequency of 3–4 days per week. Evidence category C: For advanced power training, a frequency of 4–5 days per week is recommended using predominantly total-body or upper/lower body split workouts.
summary: Recommended power training frequency is 2-3 days/week for novices, 3-4 days/week for intermediates, and 4-5 days/week for advanced trainees, using total-body or split routines.

content: Local muscular endurance, submaximal local muscular and high-intensity (or strength) endurance, has been shown to improve during resistance training (RT). RT has been shown to increase absolute LME (i.e., the maximal number of repetitions performed with a specific pretraining load). Limited effects are observed in relative LME (i.e., endurance assessed at a specific relative intensity or %1 RM).
summary: Resistance training improves local muscular endurance (LME), increasing the maximum repetitions performed with a given load, but with limited impact on relative endurance.

content: Moderate to low RT with high repetitions has been shown to be most effective for improving absolute LME in most studies, although one study found high-intensity, low-repetition training to be more effective in highly trained endurance athletes. A relationship exists between increases in strength and LME such that strength training alone may improve endurance to a certain extent. However, specificity of training produces the greatest improvements.
summary: Moderate to low-repetition resistance training is most effective for improving absolute LME, though high-intensity training may benefit highly trained athletes. Strength training alone can improve endurance, but specific training yields the best results.

content: Training to increase LME implies that the individual 1) performs high repetitions (long-duration sets with high muscle time under tension) and/or 2) minimizes recovery between sets. Exercises stressing multiple or large muscle groups have elicited the greatest acute metabolic responses during resistance exercise. Metabolic demand is an important stimulus for adaptations within skeletal muscle necessary to improve LME (increased mitochondrial and capillary number, fiber type transitions, and buffering capacity).
summary: Training for LME involves high repetitions, long sets, and minimal rest between sets. Exercises targeting multiple muscle groups maximize metabolic demand, which stimulates adaptations improving LME.

content: The sequencing of exercises may not be as important in comparison to strength training as fatigue (i.e., substrate depletion and accumulation of metabolic waste products) is a necessary component of endurance training.
summary: Exercise sequencing is less crucial for LME training compared to strength training, as fatigue from metabolic stress is a key training stimulus.

content: Evidence category A: It is recommended that unilateral and bilateral multiple- and single-joint exercises be included in a program targeting improved LME using various sequencing combinations for novice, intermediate, and advanced LME training. Light loads coupled with higher repetitions (15–25 repetitions or more) have been shown to be most effective for increasing LME. Moderate to heavy loading (coupled with short rest periods) is also effective for increasing high-intensity and absolute LME.
summary: Programs targeting LME should include unilateral and bilateral exercises, with light loads and high repetitions (15-25) being most effective. Moderate to heavy loading with short rest periods also enhances LME.

content: Evidence category A: For novice and intermediate training, it is recommended that relatively light loads be used (10–15 repetitions). Evidence category C: For advanced training, it is recommended that various loading strategies be used for multiple sets per exercise (10–25 repetitions or more) in a periodized manner leading to higher overall volume using lighter intensities.
summary: Recommended loading strategies for LME training vary by experience level. Novices and intermediates use lighter loads (10-15 reps), while advanced trainees utilize various loading strategies with higher reps (10-25) in a periodized manner.

content: The duration of rest intervals during resistance exercise appears to affect LME. It has been shown that bodybuilders (who typically train with high-volume and short rest periods) demonstrate a significantly lower fatigue rate in comparison to power lifters (who typically train with low to moderate volume and longer rest periods). These data demonstrate the benefits of high-volume, short rest period workouts for improving LME. It is important to note that another popular method of endurance training is circuit RT.
summary: Rest intervals influence LME. Bodybuilders, with their high-volume, short-rest training, exhibit lower fatigue compared to powerlifters. High-volume, short-rest workouts are beneficial for LME, and circuit training is another effective method.

content: Evidence category C: It is recommended that short rest periods be used for LME training, for example, 1–2 min for high-repetition sets (15–20 repetitions or more), 60 sec for moderate (10–15 repetitions) sets. For circuit weight training, it is recommended that rest periods correspond to the time needed to get from one exercise station to another.
summary: Recommended rest periods for LME training are short, 1-2 minutes for high reps, 60 seconds for moderate reps, and correspond to exercise station transitions in circuit training.

content: The frequency for LME training appears similar to hypertrophy training. Evidence category A: Low frequency (2–3 days per week) is effective in novice individuals when training the entire body. Evidence category B: For intermediate training, 3 days per week is recommended for total-body workouts and 4 days per week is recommended for upper/lower body split routine workouts. Evidence category C: For advanced training, a higher frequency may be used (4–6 days per week) if muscle group split routines are used.
summary: LME training frequency is similar to hypertrophy training, with 2-3 days/week for novices, 3 days for total-body or 4 days for split routines for intermediates, and potentially 4-6 days/week for advanced trainees using split routines.

content: Studies examining isokinetic exercise have shown that a fast training velocity, that is, 180 -Isj1, was more effective than a slow training velocity, that is, 30 -Isj1, for improving LME (2,186). Thus, fast contraction velocities are recommended for isokinetic training.
summary: Isokinetic studies show that faster training velocities (180°/sec) are more effective for improving LME compared to slower velocities (30°/sec). Fast contraction velocities are recommended for isokinetic training.

content: Evidence category A: Low frequency (2–3 days per week) is effective in novice individuals when training the entire body. Evidence category B: For intermediate training, 3 days per week is recommended for total-body workouts and 4 days per week is recommended for upper/lower body split routine workouts. Evidence category C: For advanced training, a higher frequency may be used (4–6 days per week) if muscle group split routines are used.
summary: This chunk outlines recommended training frequencies for different experience levels.

content: Studies examining isokinetic exercise have shown that a fast training velocity, that is, 180 -Isj1, was more effective than a slow training velocity, that is, 30 -Isj1, for improving LME (2,186). Thus, fast contraction velocities are recommended for isokinetic training. However, it appears that both fast and slow velocities are effective for improving LME during dynamic constant external RT.
summary: This chunk discusses the effectiveness of different training velocities for improving LME in isokinetic and dynamic resistance training.

content: Two effective strategies used to prolong set duration are 1) moderate repetition number using an intentionally slow velocity and 2) high repetition number using moderate to fast velocities. Ballor et al. (17) has shown that intentionally slow-velocity training with light loads (5:5 and slower ) was more metabolically demanding than moderate and fast velocities. However, Mazzetti et al. (173) showed that explosive CON repetition velocity resulted in greater rates of energy expenditure than a slower velocity (2:2). When matched for intensity and volume, slower velocity may result in greater blood lactates (173).
summary: This chunk explores strategies for prolonging set duration and the metabolic demands of different training velocities.

content: Increasing the time under tension with sufficient loading can increase muscular fatigue (262), and fatigue is important to eliciting LME enhancement. This result was shown by Tran et al. (262) who compared three sets of 10 repetitions (5:5), 10 repetitions (2:2), or 5 repetitions (10:4) and reported that the highest volume load and time under tension, for example, protocol 1, resulted in the largest magnitude of peripheral fatigue. Peak ISOM force (19%) and rate of force development (RFD) (46%) were reduced significantly more than with the other protocols (13–15% and 9–13%, respectively). Thus, traditional velocities may result in less fatigue than slower velocities provided loading is sufficient. However, it is difficult to perform a large number of repetitions using intentionally slow velocities. It is recommended that intentionally slow velocities be used when a moderate number of repetitions (10–15) are used. Moderate to fast velocities are more effective for increasing repetition number than slow-velocity training (161). If performing a large number of repetitions (15–25 or more), then moderate to faster velocities are recommended.
summary: This chunk discusses the relationship between time under tension, fatigue, and training velocity for maximizing LME.

content: Improved motor performance results from RT. The principle of Bspecificity[ is important for improving motor performance as the greatest improvements are observed when RT programs are prescribed that are specific to the task or the activity.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of specificity in resistance training for improving motor performance.

content: Force production during isokinetic and dynamic resistance exercise measures correlates to vertical jump height (28,208,216), and RT may improve vertical jump (1,252). High correlations between closed-chain exercises (exercises where the distal segments are fixed, i.e., squat) and vertical ( r =0 . 7 2 )a n ds t a n d i n gl o n gj u m p(r = 0.65) performance have been reported (24), and training with closed-chain exercises is more effective for improving vertical jump than open-chain exercises (12). Total-body multiple-joint exercises such as the Olympic lifts (snatch, clean and jerk, and variations) have been shown to improve jumping ability (82,120,263) to a greater extent than strength training (120). The high velocity and joint involvement of these exercises and their ability to integrate strength, power, and neuromuscular coordination demonstrates a direct carryover to improving jump performance.
summary: This chunk discusses the relationship between force production, exercise type, and vertical jump performance.

content: The effect of intensity on vertical jump improvements appears related to contraction velocity. Several studies (98,99,276) have shown improvements in jump height using light loads (G60% of 1 RM). Other reports show vertical jump enhancement can be achieved while using higher intensities ( 980% of 1 RM) (1). Multiple-set RT has been shown to be superior for improving vertical jump performance in comparison to single-set RT programs (149), and 5–6 d Iwkj1 of training elicited greater vertical jump improvements than 3–4 d Iwkj1 in football players (118). It is recommended that multiple-joint exercises be performed using a combination of heavy and light to moderate loading (using fast repetition velocity), with moderate to high volume in periodized fashion 4–6 d Iwkj1 for maximal progression in vertical jumping ability (1,82,98,99,120,149,263). The inclusion of plyometric training (explosive form of exercise involving various jumps) in combination with RT is recommended.
summary: This chunk explores the impact of training intensity, volume, and frequency on vertical jump performance.

content: Force production is related to sprint performance (4,11) and is a good indicator of speed when testing is performed at isokinetic velocities greater than 180 -Isj1 (205). Relative (to body mass) strength correlates highly with sprint velocity and acceleration ( r = 0.88) (208) as well as jump squat height and power (46). However, increasing maximal strength does not appear to be highly related to reducing sprint time (15). Traditional strength and ballistic training has only produced small reductions in sprint times (118,120,174). However, specific hip flexor strength training was shown to reduce sprint time (50). The combination of strength and sprint training results in the greatest improvements in sprinting speed (52). It is recommended that the combination of resistance and ballistic resistance exercise (along with sprint and plyometric training) be included for progression in sprinting ability (51,118,120,174).
summary: This chunk examines the relationship between strength, sprint performance, and training methods.

content: Muscular strength is an important factor in an individual’s ability to stop and change direction rapidly (11,119,208). Lower-body multiple-joint exercise strength and power have been shown to correlate to various agility tests (168). A significant relationship has been reported between peak ECC hamstring force at 90 -Isj1 and agility run time and may be an important indicator of success (11).
summary: This chunk highlights the connection between lower-body strength, power, and agility.

content: The combination of strength and sprint training results in the greatest improvements in sprinting speed (52). It is recommended that the combination of resistance and ballistic resistance exercise (along with sprint and plyometric training) be included for progression in sprinting ability (51,118,120,174).
summary: Combining strength and sprint training maximizes sprinting speed improvements. Resistance, ballistic, sprint, and plyometric training are recommended for sprinting progression.

content: Muscular strength is an important factor in an individual’s ability to stop and change direction rapidly (11,119,208). Lower-body multiple-joint exercise strength and power have been shown to correlate to various agility tests (168). A significant relationship has been reported between peak ECC hamstring force at 90 -Isj1 and agility run time and may be an important indicator of success (11).
summary: Muscular strength, particularly in the lower body, is crucial for rapid stopping and changing direction. Strength and power correlate with agility test performance, and hamstring strength is linked to agility run times.

content: It appears that agility-specific training is most beneficial for enhancing agility performance.
summary: Agility-specific training is most effective for improving agility.

content: Strength in the kicking limb for soccer players highly correlates to ball velocity (218). Significant correlations have been shown between wrist and elbow extensor and flexors, shoulder abduction/adduction, and shoulder internal rotation strength and throwing speed (73,204).
summary: Kicking strength in soccer players directly influences ball velocity.  Wrist, elbow, shoulder strength, and throwing speed are correlated.

content: Several studies have shown increases (2.0–4.1%) in throwing velocities in both baseball (162,180,196) and European handball (117) players following traditional (162,196) and ballistic (180) RT. Improvements in shot put performance (38), golf (261), distance running (134), swimming performance (86), and tennis service velocity (155) have been reported following RT.
summary: Resistance training, both traditional and ballistic, increases throwing velocity in baseball and handball.  RT also improves performance in shot put, golf, distance running, swimming, and tennis.

content: Progression and maintenance (maintenance of physical function in this population may be viewed as progression) in healthy, older adults is brought about by systematic manipulation of the acute program variables.
summary: Progression in healthy older adults involves systematically adjusting training variables to promote physical function improvement.

content: Caution must be taken with the elderly population as to the rate of progression, particularly those with hypertension, arthritis, cardiovascular disease, or any other debilitating condition that limits physical function. There are other modes of resistance exercise, such as aquatic resistance exercise, that have been shown to be especially beneficial in the older population and to reduce some of the risks of resistance exercise.
summary: Progression in older adults should be gradual, especially for those with health conditions. Aquatic resistance exercise is a beneficial alternative for older adults.

content: These studies have shown increased muscular strength, power, and bone mineral density as well as improvements in cardiovascular and psychological function (13,257,264,266,267). Each individual will respond differently based on their current training status and past experience, joint health, and individual response to the training stress.
summary: Resistance training improves strength, power, bone density, cardiovascular health, and psychological well-being in older adults. Individual responses vary based on training history, joint health, and stress tolerance.

content: A quality training program should improve the quality of life by enhancing several components of muscular fitness, that is, strength, balance, etc. (61). Programs that include variation, gradual progressive overload, careful attention to recovery and stress, and specificity are warranted.
summary: Effective training programs enhance quality of life by improving muscular fitness components like strength and balance.  Variation, progressive overload, recovery, and specificity are essential program elements.

content: This finding was recently shown in elderly women where peak torque and average power plateaued as a result of a significant increase in volume (at the same intensity) (243).
summary: Increased training volume, while maintaining intensity, can lead to plateaus in peak torque and average power in elderly women.

content: Muscular strength and hypertrophy training may improve the quality of life and limit sarcopenia. Optimizing strength to meet/exceed performance goals is important to a growing number of older adults.
summary: Strength training can improve quality of life and combat sarcopenia in older adults.  Meeting or exceeding performance goals is increasingly important for this population.

content: Numerous studies have shown increased muscle strength and size in older adults following RT as long as basic requirements of intensity and volume are met (31,36,61,69,77,105,106). The basic RT program recommended by the ACSM for the healthy adult (6,7) has been an effective starting point in the elderly population.
summary: Resistance training increases muscle strength and size in older adults, provided intensity and volume requirements are met. The ACSM's basic program is effective for this population.

content: When the older adult’s long-term goal is progression toward higher levels of strength and hypertrophy, evidence supports the use of variation in program design (105, 106, 154). Studies have shown significant improvements in strength in this population (61,69,77).
summary: For long-term strength and hypertrophy goals, older adults benefit from varied training programs.  Studies consistently demonstrate strength improvements in this population.

content: It is important that progression be introduced gradually. A training frequency of 1–3 dIwkj1 produced similar increases in strength; however, 3d Iwkj1 was superior to 1–2 d for improving LME, coordination, balance, and cardiorespiratory fitness in older women (193).
summary: Gradual progression is crucial. Training 3 days per week is superior to 1-2 days per week for improving strength, coordination, balance, and cardiorespiratory fitness in older women.

content: Some studies have shown similar strength increases between moderate (50–60% of 1 RM) and high (80–85% of 1 RM) training intensities or 6–15 RM (109, 265) over 18–24 wk of training. Training 3 d Iwkj1 with 50%, 65%, and 80% of 1 RM each day produced similar strength increases to training 3 d Iwkj1 with 80% of 1 RM (128).
summary: Similar strength gains can be achieved with moderate and high training intensities over time. Training with varying intensities each day can be as effective as training with a single high intensity.

content: However, several studies have shown greater strength increases with high-intensity (80–83% of 1 RM) versus moderate-intensity (50–63% of 1 RM) and low-intensity (20–48% of 1 RM) training (53,65,66,135,136). Thus, a variety of intensities may be effective in this population especially early in training.
