summary: High-intensity training generally produces greater strength gains compared to moderate and low intensities.  Early in training, a variety of intensities can be beneficial.

content: Evidence statement and recommendation.Evidence category A. For improvements in strength and hypertrophy in older adults, the use of free-weight and machine multiple- and single-joint exercises with slow to moderate lifting velocity for one to three sets per exercise with 60–80% of 1 RM for 8–12 repetitions with 1–3 min of rest in between sets for 2–3 dIwkj1 is recommended (31,53,61,65,66,69,77,105,106, 109,128,135,136,265).
summary: For older adults, the recommended training program includes free-weight and machine exercises, slow to moderate lifting velocity, 1-3 sets per exercise, 60-80% of 1RM, 8-12 repetitions, 1-3 minutes rest, and 2-3 training days per week.

content: The ability to develop high muscular power diminishes with age (107). An increase in strength and power enables the older adult to improve performance in tasks that require a rapid RFD, including a reduced risk of falls (212). There is support for the inclusion of power training for the healthy older adult (107,154).
summary: Muscular power declines with age, but strength and power training can improve performance in tasks requiring rapid force development and reduce fall risk in older adults.

content: Muscle atrophy results from fiber denervation with loss of some fibers and atrophy of others, that is, especially fast twitch, with aging and inactivity (164). Age-related muscle atrophy is associated with reductions in strength and power (77,104), and reductions in power exceed decreases in maximal strength (246).
summary: Muscle atrophy, particularly of fast-twitch fibers, occurs with aging and inactivity. This leads to age-related declines in strength and power, with power loss exceeding strength loss.

content: Although most studies in the elderly examined heavy RT programs, power training may optimize functional abilities as well as have secondary effects on other physiological systems, for example, connective tissue (18).
summary: While most elderly resistance training studies focus on heavy lifting, power training may be more beneficial for functional abilities and positively impact other physiological systems like connective tissue.

content: Progressive resistance training recommendations should include CON, ECC, and ISOM actions for novice, intermediate, and advanced training.
summary: Progressive resistance training programs should incorporate concentric, eccentric, and isometric actions for different training levels.

content: Training with loads È60–70% of 1 RM for 8–12 repetitions is recommended for novice to intermediate individuals and cycling loads of 80–100% of 1 RM for advanced individuals.
summary: Training loads for novice to intermediate individuals should be 60-70% of 1RM for 8-12 repetitions. Advanced individuals should cycle loads between 80-100% of 1RM.

content: Muscle atrophy results from fiber denervation with loss of some fibers and atrophy of others, that is, especially fast twitch, with aging and inactivity (164). Age-related muscle atrophy is associated with reductions in strength and power (77,104), and reductions in power exceed decreases in maximal strength (246).
summary: Muscle atrophy, particularly fast-twitch fibers, occurs with aging and inactivity, leading to strength and power reductions.

content: Although most studies in the elderly examined heavy RT programs, power training may optimize functional abilities as well as have secondary effects on other physiological systems, for example, connective tissue (18).
summary: Power training may be beneficial for the elderly, optimizing functional abilities and potentially impacting connective tissue.

content: Progressive resistance training recommendations should include CON, ECC, and ISOM actions for novice, intermediate, and advanced training.
summary: Progressive resistance training programs should incorporate concentric, eccentric, and isometric muscle actions for all training levels.

content: Training with loads È60–70% of 1 RM for 8–12 repetitions is recommended for novice to intermediate individuals and cycling loads of 80–100% of 1 RM for advanced individuals.
summary: Training load recommendations vary by experience level, with novice to intermediate individuals using 60-70% of 1RM for 8-12 reps, and advanced individuals cycling loads up to 100% of 1RM.

content: When training at a specific RM load, it is recommended that a 2–10% increase in load be applied when the individual can perform the current workload for 1–2 repetitions over the desired number on two consecutive training sessions.
summary: Progressive overload should be implemented by increasing weight by 2-10% when the individual can perform 1-2 more reps than the target for two consecutive sessions.

content: It is recommended that 1–3 sets per exercise be used by novice individuals.
summary: Novice individuals should perform 1-3 sets per exercise.

content: Multiple-set programs (with systematic variation of volume and intensity) are recommended for progression to intermediate and advanced training.
summary: Intermediate and advanced trainees should utilize multiple-set programs with varying volume and intensity.

content: Unilateral and bilateral single- and multiple-joint exercises should be included with emphasis on multiple-joint exercises for maximizing strength in novice, intermediate, and advanced individuals.
summary: Training programs should include both unilateral and bilateral exercises, with a focus on multi-joint movements for strength development across all experience levels.

content: Free-weight and machine exercises should be included for novice to intermediate training.
summary: Novice to intermediate training should incorporate both free-weight and machine exercises.

content: For advanced strength training, it is recommended that emphasis be placed on free-weight exercises with machine exercises used to compliment program needs.
summary: Advanced strength training should prioritize free-weight exercises, supplementing with machines as needed.

content: Recommendations for sequencing exercises for novice, intermediate, and advanced strength training include large muscle group exercises before small muscle group exercises, multiple-joint exercises before single-joint exercises, higher-intensity exercises before lower-intensity exercises, or rotation of upper and lower body or opposing exercises.
summary: Exercise sequencing recommendations include prioritizing large muscle groups, multi-joint exercises, and higher intensity movements, with options for upper/lower body or opposing muscle group rotations.

content: It is recommended that rest periods of at least 2–3 min be used for core exercises using heavier loads for novice, intermediate, and advanced training. For assistance exercises, a shorter rest period length of 1–2 min may suffice.
summary: Rest periods should be at least 2-3 minutes for heavy core exercises and 1-2 minutes for assistance exercises across all training levels.

content: For untrained individuals, it is recommended that slow and moderate CON velocities be used. For intermediate training, it is recommended that moderate CON velocity be used. For advanced training, the inclusion of a continuum of velocities from unintentionally slow to fast CON velocities is recommended and should correspond to the intensity.
summary: Concentric velocity recommendations vary by experience level, with untrained individuals using slow to moderate speeds, intermediate individuals using moderate speeds, and advanced individuals incorporating a range of velocities based on intensity.

content: It is recommended that novice individuals train the entire body 2–3 d Iwkj1. It is recommended that for progression to intermediate training, a frequency of 3–4 d Iwkj1 be used (based on how many muscle groups are trained per workout). It is recommended that advanced lifters train 4–6 d Iwkj1.
summary: Training frequency recommendations increase with experience level, from 2-3 days per week for novices to 4-6 days per week for advanced lifters.

content: It is recommended that CON, ECC, and ISOM muscle actions be included for muscle hypertrophy.
summary: Muscle hypertrophy training should incorporate all three muscle actions: concentric, eccentric, and isometric.

content: For novice and intermediate training, it is recommended that moderate loading be used (70–85% of 1 RM) for 8–12 repetitions per set for 1–3 sets per exercise. For advanced training, it is recommended that a loading range of 70–100% of 1 RM be used for 1–12 repetitions per set for 3–6 sets per exercise in a periodized manner such that the majority of training is devoted to 6–12 RM and less training devoted to 1–6 RM loading.
summary: Training load recommendations vary by experience level, with novice and intermediate individuals using 70-85% of 1RM for 8-12 reps, and advanced individuals utilizing a wider range (70-100% of 1RM) with a periodized approach emphasizing 6-12RM.

content: It is recommended that single- and multiple-joint free-weight and machine exercises be included in novice, intermediate, and advanced individuals.
summary: Training programs across all experience levels should include both single and multiple-joint exercises using free weights and machines.

content: For exercise sequencing, an order similar to strength training is recommended.
summary: Exercise sequencing for power training should resemble that of strength training.

content: It is recommended that 1- to 2-min rest periods be used in novice and intermediate training; for advanced training, length of rest period should correspond to the goals of each exercise such that 2- to 3-min rest periods may be used with heavy loading for core exercises and 1–2 min may be used for other exercises of moderate to moderately high intensity.
summary: Rest periods should be 1-2 minutes for novice and intermediate training, and 2-3 minutes for heavy core exercises in advanced training, with shorter rest periods for other exercises.

content: It is recommended that slow to moderate velocities be used by novice- and intermediate-trained individuals; for advanced training, it is recommended that slow, moderate, and fast repetition velocities be used depending on the load, repetition number, and goals of the particular exercise.
summary: Repetition velocity recommendations vary by experience level, with novices and intermediates using slow to moderate speeds, and advanced individuals incorporating a range of speeds based on load, reps, and exercise goals.

content: It is recommended that a frequency of 2–3 d Iwkj1 be used for novice training. For intermediate training, the recommendation is similar for total-body workouts or 4 d Iwkj1 when using an upper/lower body split routine. For advanced training, a frequency of 4–6 d Iwkj1 is recommended.
summary: Training frequency recommendations increase with experience level, from 2-3 days per week for novices to 4-6 days per week for advanced lifters.

content: The use of predominately multiple-joint exercises performed with sequencing guidelines similar to strength training is recommended for novice, intermediate, and advanced power training.
summary: Power training programs should emphasize multiple-joint exercises and follow a similar sequencing approach as strength training.

content: It is recommended that concurrent to a typical strength training program, a power component is incorporated consisting of 1–3 sets per exercise using light to moderate loading (30–60% of 1 RM for upper body exercises, 0–60% of 1 RM for lower body exercises) for 3–6 repetitions not to failure.
summary: Power training should be incorporated alongside strength training, using light to moderate loads (30-60% of 1RM) for 3-6 reps per exercise.

content: Various loading strategies are recommended for advanced training. Heavy loading (85–100% of 1 RM) is necessary for increasing force and light to moderate loading (30–60% of 1 RM for upper body exercises, 0–60% of 1 RM for lower body exercises) performed at an explosive velocity is necessary for increasing fast force production. A multiple-set (3–6 sets) power program be integrated into a strength training program consisting of 1–6 repetitions in a periodized manner is recommended.
summary: Advanced power training requires a combination of heavy loading for force development and light to moderate loading for explosive power, with a periodized approach incorporating 1-6 reps per set.

content: Rest periods of at least 2–3 min between sets for core exercises are recommended when intensity is high. For assistance exercises and those of less intensity, a shorter rest interval (1–2 min) is recommended.
summary: Rest periods should be at least 2-3 minutes for high-intensity core exercises and 1-2 minutes for assistance exercises.

content: The recommended frequency for novice power training is similar to strength training (2–3 d Iwkj1). For intermediate power training, it is recommended that either a total-body or upper/lower–body split workout be used for a frequency of 3–4 d Iwkj1. For advanced power training, a frequency of 4–5 d Iwkj1 is recommended using predominantly total-body or upper/lower–body split workouts.
summary: Power training frequency increases with experience level, similar to strength training, ranging from 2-3 days per week for novices to 4-5 days per week for advanced lifters.

content: It is recommended that unilateral and bilateral multiple- and single-joint exercises be included using various sequencing combinations for novice, intermediate, and advanced local muscular endurance training.
summary: Local muscular endurance training should incorporate both unilateral and bilateral exercises across multiple joint types, with varied sequencing.

content: For novice and intermediate training, it is recommended that relatively light loads be used (10–15 repetitions) with moderate to high volume. For advanced training, it is recommended that various loading strategies be used for multiple sets per exercise (10–25 repetitions or more) in a periodized manner leading to a higher overall volume using lighter intensities.
summary: Local muscular endurance training utilizes relatively light loads (10-15 reps) with moderate to high volume for novices and intermediates, while advanced training incorporates various loading strategies for higher reps (10-25+) in a periodized manner.

content: It is recommended that short rest periods be used for muscular endurance training, e.g., 1–2 min for high-repetition sets (15–20 repetitions or more), less than 1 minute for moderate (10–15 repetitions) sets. For circuit weight training, it is recommended that rest periods correspond to the time needed to get from one exercise station to another.
summary: Rest periods for muscular endurance training should be short, ranging from 1-2 minutes for high-rep sets to less than 1 minute for moderate reps. Circuit training utilizes rest periods based on transition time between exercises.

content: For novice and intermediate training, it is recommended that relatively light loads be used (10–15 repetitions) with moderate to high volume.  Similar recommendations may apply to older adults as young adults, e.g., low to moderate loads performed for moderate to high repetitions (10–15 or more) for enhancing muscular endurance.  Similar recommendations may apply to older adults as young adults, for example, low to moderate loads (40–70% of 1 RM) performed for moderate to high repetitions (10–15 or more) (81).
summary: Recommendations for novice, intermediate, and older adult training emphasize moderate to high repetitions with moderate to light loads for muscular endurance.

content: For advanced training, it is recommended that various loading strategies be used for multiple sets per exercise (10–25 repetitions or more) in a periodized manner leading to a higher overall volume using lighter intensities.  For advanced power training, a frequency of 4–5 d Iwkj1 is recommended using predominantly total-body or upper/lower–body split workouts.  For advanced training, a higher frequency may be used (4–6 d Iwkj1) if muscle group split routines are used.
summary: Advanced training involves periodized loading strategies with higher volume and frequency, potentially using muscle group splits.

content: It is recommended that short rest periods be used for muscular endurance training, e.g., 1–2 min for high-repetition sets (15–20 repetitions or more), less than 1 minute for moderate (10–15 repetitions) sets. For circuit weight training, it is recommended that rest periods correspond to the time needed to get from one exercise station to another.
summary: Rest periods for muscular endurance training should be short, depending on repetition range, and circuit training utilizes rest periods for transition between exercises.

content: It is recommended that intentionally slow velocities be used when a moderate number of repetitions (10–15) are used. If performing a large number of repetitions (15–25 or more), then moderate to faster velocities are recommended.
summary: Training velocity should be adjusted based on repetition range, with slower velocities for moderate repetitions and faster velocities for higher repetitions.

content: It is recommended that multiple-joint exercises be performed using a combination of heavy and light to moderate loading (using fast repetition velocity) with moderate to high volume in periodized fashion 4–6 d Iwkj1 for maximal progression in vertical jumping ability. The inclusion of plyometric training (explosive form of exercise involving various jumps) in combination with resistance training is recommended.
summary: For maximizing vertical jump ability, combine multiple-joint exercises with varying loads and high volume, incorporating plyometric training.

content: It is recommended that the combination of heavy resistance and ballistic resistance exercise (along with sprint and plyometric training) be included for progression in sprinting ability.
summary: To improve sprinting ability, combine heavy resistance training with ballistic exercises, sprints, and plyometrics.

content: For further improvements in strength and hypertrophy in older adults, the use of both multiple- and single-joint exercises (free weights and machines) with slow-to-moderate lifting velocity, for 1–3 sets per exercise with 60–80% of 1 RM for 8–12 repetitions with 1–3 min of rest in between sets for 2–3 d Iwkj1 is recommended.
summary: Older adults can benefit from a combination of multiple and single-joint exercises with moderate loads and rest periods for strength and hypertrophy gains.

content: Increasing power in healthy older adults include: 1) training to improve muscular strength, and 2) the performance of both single- and multiple-joint exercises for 1–3 sets per exercise using light to moderate loading (30–60% of 1 RM) for 6–10 repetitions with high repetition velocity (29,53,57,70,107,115,124,185,199,200,243).
summary: Improving power in older adults involves both strength training and exercises with light to moderate loads and high repetition velocity.

content: Low frequency (2–3 d Iwkj1) is effective in novice individuals when training the entire body. For intermediate training, 3 d Iwkj1 is recommended for total-body workouts and 4 d Iwkj1 is recommended for upper/lower body split routine workouts.
summary: Training frequency recommendations vary based on experience level, with novices benefiting from lower frequency and intermediates utilizing 3-4 days per week.

content: Improvements in LME in the older adult may lead to an enhanced ability to perform submaximal work and recreational activities. Although studies examining LME training in the older adult are limited, LME may be enhanced by circuit RT (275), strength training (125), and high repetition, moderate-load programs (9) in younger populations.
summary: Improving local muscular endurance in older adults can enhance daily activities. While research is limited, circuit training, strength training, and high repetition, moderate-load programs may be beneficial.

content: Multiple-set training led to 44.3–60.5% increases in LME, whereas single-set training led approximately 10% increases in individuals 65–78 yr of age (81).
summary: Multiple-set training is more effective than single-set training for increasing local muscular endurance in older adults.

content: Low-to-moderate intensity (20–80% of 1 RM), high-velocity training has been tolerable in the elderly population and consistently shown to enhance power production, strength, and performance of activities of daily living, for example, chair rise, and balance.
summary: High-velocity training with moderate intensity is safe and effective for improving power, strength, and functional performance in older adults.

content: In comparison to traditional RT, power training has been shown to produce similar (29,70) and inferior increases (185) in maximal strength, greater improvements in power (29,70), and greater functional performance enhancement (29,185). de Vos et al. (53) reported that power training with 50% of subjects’ 1 RM led to the highest gains in muscle power, whereas RT with loads corresponding to 80% of subjects’ 1 RM led to the highest gains in muscular strength and endurance.
summary: Power training generally leads to greater improvements in power and functional performance compared to traditional resistance training, although maximal strength gains may be similar or lower.

content: Evidence category B . Increasing power in healthy older adults include 1) training to improve muscular strength and 2) the performance of both single- and multiple-joint exercises for one tot three sets per exercise us ing light to moderate loading (30–60% of 1 RM) for 6–10 repetitions with high repetition velocity (29,53,57,70,107,115,124,185,199,200,243).
summary: Evidence supports increasing power in older adults through strength training and exercises with light to moderate loads and high repetition velocity.
